column1: ,True,,,6,0,0,NO_ATTACK,6,A1 authenticates A2 on N5,N5 is encrypted under K7, which is freshly established via the trusted server a3 and only known to A1, A2, and a3. A1 decrypts {|N5|}K7 using its copy of K7, verifying that only A2 (or a3) could have produced that ciphertext. The freshness of K7 prevents replay, and the intruder lacks shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3) to subvert key distribution.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,1,NO_ATTACK,6,A2 authenticates A1 on N5,A2 generates N5 and sends {|N5|}K7 to A1. A1 responds with {|pre(N5)|}K7. Since pre is a public function, only an agent who knows N5 (i.e., A1 after decrypting) can compute pre(N5). The encryption under K7 ensures the response is fresh and bound to the session. The intruder cannot forge this without K7 or N5.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,2,NO_ATTACK,6,N5 secret between A1,A2","N5 is transmitted only in encrypted form under K7, which is known exclusively to A1, A2, and the trusted server a3. The underlying symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3) protect K7 during distribution. The intruder cannot decrypt the messages or obtain K7, so N5 remains confidential.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,3,NO_ATTACK,6,A2 *->* A1: N5,This goal expresses that A2 sends N5 to A1 over an insecure channel. The message {|N5|}K7 is encrypted, ensuring integrity and origin (only A2 knows K7). The intruder cannot modify or forge the ciphertext without detection. Combined with the key freshness, A1 receives exactly N5 as sent by A2.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,4,NO_ATTACK,6,A1 authenticates a3 on K7,A1 receives {|N4, A2, K7, {|K7,N6,A1|}shk(A2,a3)|}shk(A1,a3). A1 decrypts and checks that N4 matches its freshly generated nonce. Only a3 possesses shk(A1,a3) to construct this encryption. The inclusion of N4 prevents replay. Thus, A1 is assured that a3 generated K7.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,5,NO_ATTACK,6,A2 authenticates a3 on K7,A2 receives {|K7, N6, A1|}shk(A2,a3) and decrypts using its shared key with a3. It verifies that N6 matches its own nonce from step 2. Only a3 could have produced this ciphertext because it knows shk(A2,a3). The freshness of N6 ensures the message is not a replay, so A2 trusts that a3 issued K7.",,,,

column2: A1 weakly authenticates A2 on ccn(A2,A1)

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates A2 on ccn(A2,A1)","The message from A2 is signed with inv(sk(A2)) and encrypted for A1. A1 decrypts and verifies the signature, linking ccn(A2,A1) to A2. The intruder cannot forge the signature, so any accepted message originates from A2. Replay does not violate weak authentication as it only requires the value was sent by A2 at some point.",,,,

column2: A1 weakly authenticates A2 on ccn(A2,A1)

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,ccn(A2,A1) secret between A2,A1","The value ccn(A2,A1) is encrypted with pk(A1) after being signed. Only A1 can decrypt with inv(pk(A1)). Neither A3 nor the intruder possess inv(pk(A1)), so they cannot learn the secret. The key ccn(A2,A1) is initially known only to A2 and A1, and no other agent can obtain it from the protocol messages.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,The protocol only contains a signed message from A1 to A2 containing N3, SQNx1, and A2. A2 can verify the signature using sk(A1), but there is no freshness mechanism such as a challenge-response or stateful check of the sequence number. The intruder can intercept the signed message and replay it later. Since A2 does not verify freshness (e.g., by checking that SQNx1 is new or by requiring a fresh challenge), the replayed message will be accepted, causing A2 to authenticate A1 on an old N3. This constitutes a replay attack on the authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,The protocol binds the signature {A2,N3}inv(sk(A1)) to the current session through two mechanisms: (1) the Diffie-Hellman shared secret exp(exp(g,N5),N6) protects the third message, and (2) the inclusion of the fresh nonce N4 from A2 inside the symmetric encryption provides a liveness test. Only the legitimate A1 can decrypt the second message to obtain N4 and can create the signature. The intruder cannot forge the signature nor obtain N4, so replay or impersonation attacks fail. The authentication goal holds in the symbolic model.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4
a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4,Message 1 is encrypted with pk(A2) and contains A1's signature on (N4, A2, a3). Only A2 can decrypt, and the signature verifies with sk(A1). This ensures A1 originated the signed data. No type-flaw or replay allows forgery of the signature, so weak authentication holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4
a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N4,Message 2 is encrypted with pk(a3) and carries the same signature from A1 on (N4, A2, a3). a3 decrypts and verifies the signature, which includes a3's identity. Since the signature cannot be forged and the message cannot be replayed in a way that deceives a3 (the intended recipient is bound by the identity), weak authentication holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4
a3 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2,a3","The nonce N4 is always transmitted inside asymmetric encryption (first pk(A2), then pk(a3)). Only the holders of the corresponding private keys (A2 and a3) can decrypt. The intruder cannot obtain N4 from any messages; no other transmission leaks N4.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 ->* A1: N5,The protocol does not bind N5 to any fresh challenge or session-specific value. An intruder can replay an old fourth message {|N5|}K (from a previous run) to A1, who will accept it as coming from A2 because it decrypts correctly with the shared key. There is no freshness check for N5, so A1 believes it is receiving a new N5 from A2, violating the authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 *->* A1: N5,N5 is encrypted under the long-term symmetric key shk(A1,A2), which is not known to the intruder. The intruder cannot decrypt the message to learn N5, nor can it forge a new encryption. Since no previous message reveals N5 and the key remains secret, the confidentiality of N5 is preserved.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates A2 on N5,The final message {|N5|}shk(A1,A2) is not bound to the nonces used earlier in the protocol (N3, N4). An intruder can replay an encrypted message from a previous session (e.g., the third message {|succ(N4)|}shk(A1,A2) from an earlier run) as the fourth message in a new session. A1 will decrypt it and accept the resulting value as N5, believing it came from A2 in the current run, thus violating the authentication on N5.",,,,

column2: A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5
A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6

column1: ,True,,,6,0,0,ATTACK,1,A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5","The authentication of A1 relies on the signature {A3,N9,N5,{hash(P16),N4,n14,n15,N7,N11,N12}inv(sk(A3))}inv(P17) which uses the private key inv(P17). However, P17 is not bound to A1's identity; it is sent in the clear in step 11 (A1 -> A3: P17, ...) without any certificate or long-term key linkage. An intruder can generate his own key pair, replace P17 with his own public key, and produce a valid signature using his own private key. When A3 receives the signature along with the intruder's P17, he will verify the signature successfully and falsely believe that the values N9,N5 originated from A1, thus violating weak authentication.",,,,

column2: A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5
A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6

column1: ,True,,,6,0,1,ATTACK,1,A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6","Similarly, the authentication of A2 depends on the signature {A3,N10,N6,{hash(P16),N4,n14,n15,N7,N11,N12}inv(sk(A3))}inv(P18). The public key P18 is not authenticated to A2; it is sent without proof of ownership. An intruder can generate his own key pair, supply his own P18, and sign arbitrary values. Since A3 verifies the signature using the provided P18, the intruder can impersonate A2 and make A3 accept N10,N6 as coming from A2, breaking weak authentication.",,,,

column2: A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5
A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6

column1: ,True,,,6,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,N9,N10 secret between A3,A1,A2","The nonces N9 and N10 appear only inside signed messages that are transmitted over secure channels (indicated by *->*): step 5 (A1*->*A2), step 6 (A2*->*A1), and step 7 ([A1]*->*A3). In the Dolev-Yao symbolic model, secure channels provide both confidentiality and sender authentication. The intruder cannot read the messages sent over these channels, and thus cannot learn N9 or N10. Since the values are never sent in plaintext over open channels, secrecy holds.",,,,

column2: A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5
A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6

column1: ,True,,,6,0,3,ATTACK,1,A3 weakly authenticates [A1] on N9,N5,N10,N6","This combined authentication suffers from the same key-binding flaw as the individual ones. The protocol does not link the public keys P17 and P18 to the claimed identities A1 or A2. An intruder can generate his own key pairs, supply his own P17 and P18 in the open messages (steps 11,12), and produce valid signatures for the entire tuple {N9,N5,N10,N6}. A3, verifying with the intruder's keys, will accept the values as originating from A1, violating weak authentication on all four nonces.",,,,

column2: A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5
A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6

column1: ,True,,,6,0,4,ATTACK,1,A3 weakly authenticates [A1] on N9,N5","This goal is a subset of the previous combined authentication; the same key-binding issue applies. The signature using inv(P17) is unbound to A1's identity. An intruder can impersonate A1 by replacing P17 with his own public key and signing N9,N5. A3, upon verifying with the intruder's P17, will mistakenly attribute the values to A1, leading to an attack.",,,,

column2: A3 weakly authenticates A1 on N9,N5
A3 weakly authenticates A2 on N10,N6

column1: ,True,,,6,0,5,NO_ATTACK,1,A3 weakly authenticates [A1] on P17,A3 receives P17 from A1 in step 7 via a secure channel ([A1]*->*A3). In the symbolic model, the secure channel ensures that the message originates from A1 (sender authentication) and is confidential. Therefore, when A3 obtains P17 as part of that message, he can safely attribute its origin to A1. No later open-channel transmission of P17 undermines this because weak authentication only requires existence of a matching send event; the secure channel send event already provides that causality. Hence the goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,a4 *->* A1: N7,The intruder, acting as a2, can intercept message 4 from a3 to A1 and decrypt {|K13,N6,N9,a4|}K12 using K12 (known to a2). This reveals K13. Then the intruder intercepts message 5 from A1 to a4 and decrypts {|A1,N10|}K13 to obtain N10. With K13 and N10, the intruder can forge message 6, sending {|N10|}K13 and {|n11,N7'}K13 for an arbitrary N7', thereby impersonating a4 to A1. Thus, A1 cannot authenticate a4 on N7.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on K5,The protocol uses a challenge-response with asymmetric encryption. A2 sends a nonce N4 encrypted with A1's public key. A1 responds with f(N4), a fresh nonce N3, its identity, and the new symmetric key K5, all encrypted with A2's public key. This ensures that only A1 could have produced the response because only A1 can decrypt the first message to retrieve N4 and then compute f(N4). The binding of A1's identity and K5 inside the encryption guarantees to A2 that K5 originates from A1. There is no way for an attacker to impersonate A1 without knowing inv(pk(A1)), and replays are defeated by the freshness of N4 and N3. Therefore, the authentication goal holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,4,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N3,The protocol sends N3 as part of the message 'A1 *->* A2: N4,N3' over a secure channel. The secure channel ensures confidentiality and authentication, so N3 is delivered exactly as specified, satisfying the goal.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,4,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,A2 generates a fresh nonce N4 and sends it to A1 over an authentic channel. A1 replies with N4 and N3 over a secure channel. A2 verifies the returned N4 matches the sent value, confirming that A1 is alive and has sent N3. The authentic and secure channels prevent the intruder from impersonating A1 or manipulating the nonce.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,4,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,Weak authentication only requires that if A2 believes N3 came from A1, then it actually did. Since the secure channel guarantees the origin of the message, A2 knows that N3 was sent by A1. The intruder cannot forge a secure channel message from A1.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,4,0,3,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is transmitted exclusively within the secure channel (*->*) from A1 to A2. The intruder cannot read the contents of secure channel messages. No other protocol step reveals N3, so its confidentiality is preserved.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N4,The message is sent in plaintext without integrity protection. An intruder can intercept and modify the payload (N4) or inject a message impersonating A1, so A2 cannot verify the origin or integrity of N4.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,A2 sends a fresh nonce N5 to A1, expecting it back. However, since the response (N5,N4) is in plaintext, an intruder can intercept N5 and reply with the same N5 and any N4, impersonating A1. No cryptographic binding prevents this real-time attack.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,2,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4,Weak authentication is also violated because the intruder can successfully impersonate A1 by responding to the challenge N5 with the correct N5 found in the previous message. A2 cannot distinguish the impersonator from the real A1, as no secret or signature is used.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,3,ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is sent in cleartext as part of message 3 (A1 → A2: N5,N4). The intruder, who controls the network, can eavesdrop and learn N4. Therefore, the secrecy goal is trivially broken.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N4,The message N4 is sent in plaintext from A1 to A2 as part of the composite message (N5, N4) in the third action. The intruder can impersonate A1 by sending N4 (or a fresh N4) to A2, as there is no authentication or integrity protection. A2 has no means to verify the origin of N4, so the intended transmission goal is violated.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,No cryptographic primitives are used; N4 is sent in clear. The intruder can intercept the message or generate a new N4 and send it to A2 claiming to be A1. Without any binding to A1's identity (e.g., a digital signature or shared secret), A2 cannot authenticate A1 as the origin of N4.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,2,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4,Weak authentication also fails because the intruder can freely inject N4 into any session. The lack of freshness or cryptographic guarantee allows replay or impersonation attacks. Since N4 is unprotected, A2 cannot even rely on weak evidence of A1's involvement.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,4,0,3,ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is transmitted in cleartext over the network in the third action (A1 -> A2: N5, N4). The intruder is assumed to have full network access and can eavesdrop, thus learning N4. Additionally, N4 is also sent by A2 to A3 later, further exposing it. Therefore, secrecy is trivially broken.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The message N3 is sent in cleartext without any cryptographic protection (encryption, signature, or MAC). An intruder can intercept N3 and replay it to A2, impersonating A1. Weak authentication fails because A2 cannot distinguish whether N3 genuinely originated from A1 or was replayed by the intruder.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The protocol sends the nonce N3 in plaintext without any cryptographic protection. An intruder can impersonate A1 by simply sending an arbitrary nonce to A2, as there is no mechanism (signature, MAC, or encryption binding the message to A1) for A2 to verify the origin. Hence, an attack on weak authentication is trivial.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,The message N3 is sent in cleartext over an insecure channel. The intruder can intercept the message and either drop it, modify it, or replay it. Since there is no cryptographic protection (no encryption, MAC, or signature), the intruder can easily prevent A2 from receiving the genuine N3 or can impersonate A1 by sending a different nonce. Therefore, the reachability/integrity goal is violated.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The message N3 is sent in cleartext without any cryptographic protection (no signature, HMAC, or encryption). An intruder can intercept the message and replay it, or simply generate a fresh N3 and send it to A2 claiming to be A1. Since A2 has no means to verify the origin or freshness of N3, the intruder can trivially impersonate A1, breaking weak authentication.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2",The nonce N3 is transmitted in plaintext over the network in the only protocol message. Any intruder monitoring the channel can directly read N3. There is no encryption or other confidentiality mechanism protecting N3. Hence the secrecy goal is violated.,,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K7,A1","A2 receives the ticket {|K7, N5, A1|}shk(A2,a3) directly from a3. Since only a3 and A2 know shk(A2,a3), the encryption guarantees a3's origin. The nonce N5 provides freshness, preventing replay. The intruder cannot forge a valid ticket without the key, so authentication holds.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K7,A2","A1 receives the ticket {|N4, A2, K7|}shk(A1,a3) which is encrypted with the long-term key shared with a3. Only a3 can create such a message. The nonce N4 ensures freshness and binds the ticket to the current session. Although forwarded by A2, the encryption proves a3's involvement; the intruder cannot modify or forge it.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K7 secret between A1,A2,a3","K7 is transmitted only within ciphertexts encrypted with the symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are unknown to the intruder. It is also used to encrypt nonces N4 and N6, but this does not reveal the key. No other message leaks K7. In the symbolic model, the intruder cannot decrypt these ciphertexts, so secrecy is preserved.",,,,

column2: A1 weakly authenticates A2 on ccn(A2,A1)

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates A2 on ccn(A2,A1)","The message is signed with A2's private signing key and encrypted with A1's public key. The signature binds the value ccn(A2,A1) to A2's identity, and only A1 can decrypt. Replay does not break weak authentication because the original send exists. No forgery is possible without A2's signing key.",,,,

column2: A1 weakly authenticates A2 on ccn(A2,A1)

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,ccn(A2,A1) secret between A2,A1","The value ccn(A2,A1) is transmitted only inside an encryption with A1's public key. Only A1 can decrypt. The intruder cannot learn the secret as long as the encryption is not broken. The signature does not reveal the content. No other message leaks the secret.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,The DH exponentials g^N4 and g^N5 are exchanged with digital signatures using inv(sk(A1)) and inv(sk(A2)), binding them to the respective agents. The shared secret exp(exp(g,N5),N4) is thus only known to A1 and A2. The third message encrypts N3 under this fresh session key, so A2 can decrypt it and deduce it came from A1, providing authentication. No reflection or replay attack is possible because the key is session-specific and signed messages prevent impersonation.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is encrypted symmetrically with the DH session key exp(exp(g,N5),N4). This key is computed from N4 and N5, which are known only to A1 and A2 respectively, and the signed exchange prevents a man-in-the-middle from learning the exponentials' exponents. In the symbolic model, the intruder cannot derive g^(N4*N5) from g^N4 and g^N5, so N3 remains confidential.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is sent in cleartext in message 3 as part of the signed token {N3, A2}inv(sk(A1)). The signature provides no confidentiality, so the intruder can directly observe N3 from this message.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,A2 receives a valid signature from A1 on (N3, A2) in message 3, and later a signed message 5 that proves knowledge of the Diffie-Hellman key g^(N4*N5) and binds N3 to that key. The intruder cannot forge A1's signature, and replay of old signed messages fails because the DH key differs per session. Therefore, A2 reliably concludes that A1 sent N3.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","The Diffie-Hellman exchange is not authenticated; an active adversary can perform a man-in-the-middle attack by intercepting and replacing the encrypted DH values. The adversary establishes separate DH keys with A1 and A2, decrypts the symmetric encryption of N3 from A1, learns N3, and forwards the re-encrypted message to A2, thus violating secrecy.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,Due to the same MITM attack, the adversary can impersonate A1 to A2. The adversary receives the HMAC from A2 and can generate a valid HMAC on the same (N3, A1) using the key shared with A2, causing A2 to believe that A1 originated N3, while in fact the adversary relayed it. Thus, authentication fails.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: fun2(A1,N3)","The protocol never includes a message from A1 to A2 that consists solely of fun2(A1,N3). A2 sends fun2(A1,N3) to A1 in step 2, and A1 later sends fun5(A2,fun2(A1,N3)) and fun5(A1,fun2(A1,N3)), but neither step delivers the exact term to A2. Since the goal event is required but never occurs in any honest execution, a symbolic model checker (e.g., OFMC) would trivially find a violation: the goal is not achievable as specified.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K10

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,2,K10 secret between A1,A2","K10 is encrypted under A2's public key pk(A2) in the third message. Only A2 possesses the corresponding private decryption key inv(pk(A2)). No other agent (including the intruder) can decrypt and learn K10. The signature {K10,N4,A2}inv(sk(A1)) does not reveal K10 because it is inside the encryption. Therefore, the secrecy goal holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K10

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,2,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K10,A2 can verify the certificate {A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)) using its knowledge of sk(a3) to obtain A1's public verification key sk(A1). Then it can verify the signature {K10,N4,A2}inv(sk(A1)) inside the encrypted part. This proves that the message originated from A1. The intruder cannot forge the certificate or the signature because it lacks inv(sk(a3)) and inv(sk(A1)). Replaying the message does not break weak authentication since it only requires that the message was sent by A1 at some point. Thus, no attack.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: K10,The protocol lacks any freshness mechanism (e.g., a challenge nonce from A2) to prevent replay attacks. An intruder can intercept the message in step 3 from a previous run and replay it to A2, who will decrypt and accept the old key K10 as coming from A1. Therefore, the authentication and freshness required by the goal are violated.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,N8,a3","The intruder cannot forge a3's signature, which is essential for A2 to complete authentication. To produce a valid message 5, the intruder needs N5 from a3's response {N6,N5}pk(A1), which requires A1's private key to decrypt. Additionally, the signature includes N7, which is a fresh value from A2's message 2, preventing replay or interleaving attacks. Thus, A2's belief that A1 is present with the claimed N4,N8 and a3 involvement is justified.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,a3 authenticates A1 on N8,a3,A2","For a3 to authenticate A1, it must receive a valid signature from A2 (message 6) that matches the nonces it used. The intruder cannot obtain A2's signature on hash(a3,N5,N7) without completing the protocol as A1 towards A2, which requires decrypting {N6,N5}pk(A1) to obtain N5. Without A1's private key, this is impossible. Hence, a3 only receives the expected signature from a genuine run, securing authentication.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,N4,N8 secret between A1,A2","N4 appears only in messages 1 and 2, both encrypted with the public keys of the intended recipients (pk(A2) and pk(A1)). N8 appears additionally in message 3, encrypted with pk(a3). The intruder does not possess the corresponding private keys (inv(pk(A1)), inv(pk(A2)), inv(pk(a3))), so he cannot decrypt these ciphertexts. The hash in the signature does not reveal N8. Thus, secrecy of N4 and N8 is maintained.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4
A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4,The first message is signed with A1's private signing key and encrypted with A2's public key. The signature binds N4 to A1, A2, and A3. Only A1 could have created this signature. Replay of the encrypted message does not violate weak authentication because it still originates from A1. No type-flaw or reflection is possible due to structured fields and distinct types.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4
A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5,The second message follows the same structure as the first, signed with A1's private key and encrypted for A2. The signature guarantees that A1 originated N5. Weak authentication does not require freshness, so replay does not break the goal. The protocol ensures that only A1 can produce a valid signature for N5.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A3: N4,The signed message {N4,A2,A3}inv(sk(A1)) is transmitted in cleartext from A2 to A3 (step 2) without any freshness mechanism (e.g., challenge nonce or timestamp). An intruder can capture this message and replay it to A3 at a later time, causing A3 to believe it is a fresh transmission from A1. Since A3 cannot detect the replay, the authentication goal that A3 receives N4 from A1 in a current run is violated. OFMC would model this as a replay attack.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A3: hash(N4,hash(hmac(N4,Hx1A3N4)))","The goal is a secrecy goal: the hash value should remain confidential between A1 and A3. The hash is embedded in a payload that is first signed with A1's private key and then encrypted with A3's public key. An attacker cannot decrypt the ciphertext without A3's private key and cannot forge the signature without A1's private key. The hash itself depends on a secret nonce N4 and a shared HmacKey Hx1A3N4, both presumably known only to A1 and A3, further ensuring secrecy. No attack exists.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,The authentication relies on A2's fresh nonce N4 encrypted under A1's public encryption key in message 2, and A1's signature on (N4, A2, N3) in message 3. Only A1 can decrypt N4 and produce a valid signature. A replay of an old signature fails because the included N4 is fresh per session. No man-in-the-middle or parallel session attack can force A2 to accept a forged signature, as the intruder cannot obtain A1's signing key or decrypt N4 to produce a matching signature.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,inv(pk(A1)) secret between A1,The private decryption key inv(pk(A1)) is used only by A1 to decrypt message 2. It is never transmitted or derivable from protocol messages, as the protocol only involves encryption with the public key and signing with the other private key. No message reveals the key or allows its extraction.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,inv(sk(A1)) secret between A1,The private signing key inv(sk(A1)) is used only by A1 to produce the signature in message 3. It is never transmitted or indirectly revealed by the signature, as signatures do not leak the signing key. The protocol does not contain any operation that could expose the key.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is sent only in message 3, encrypted with A2's public key (pk(A2)). Only A2 possesses the corresponding private key (inv(pk(A2))) to decrypt, so the intruder cannot obtain N3. No other message leaks N3. Hence confidentiality holds.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,In message 3, A1 signs {N4,A2,N3} with inv(sk(A1)). A2 decrypts with inv(pk(A2)), verifies the signature with sk(A1), and checks that N4 matches the nonce it sent in message 2. This binds the response to A2's challenge and confirms A1 originated N3. No replay or reflection attack succeeds because the intruder cannot forge the signature or learn N4 (encrypted in message 2).",,,,

,True,,,4,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,inv(pk(A1)) secret between A1,inv(pk(A1)) is A1's private decryption key. It is never transmitted in any message. The only operation using it is decryption of message 2, which does not reveal the key. Thus, it remains known only to A1.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,3,NO_ATTACK,1,inv(sk(A1)) secret between A1,inv(sk(A1)) is A1's private signing key. It is used only to sign the inner data in message 3, which is then encrypted. The signature itself does not disclose the key, and the encryption prevents the intruder from even seeing the raw signature. Hence the key remains secret to A1.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 ->* A1: N3,The protocol lacks authentication of the public key. An intruder can perform a man-in-the-middle attack by intercepting A1's public key P4 and replacing it with its own public key P_I. A2 then encrypts N3 with P_I, which the intruder can decrypt to obtain N3. The intruder can then re-encrypt N3 with the original P4 (if known) and send it to A1, making A1 believe the nonce came from A2. Since A1 has no means to verify the origin of the encrypted nonce, the authenticity goal fails.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The protocol does not bind A1's public key P5 to its identity. An active intruder can intercept the first message, replace P5 with its own public key PI, and then respond to A2's nonce n4 with a signed message {A2, N3'}inv(PI) using its own private key. A2 verifies this signature with the received public key PI, thereby accepting the intruder as A1 and believing that A1 authenticated on N3'. Since A1 never participated, the weak authentication goal is violated.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *-> A2: N3,A1 sends {A2,N3}inv(sk(A1)) which A2 verifies with sk(A1). This signature is bound to the session because it is included (via hash) inside the symmetric encryption keyed with the Diffie-Hellman shared secret exp(exp(g,N5),N6). The DH key is fresh per session (depends on fresh N5 and N6), so replay of the signed part fails due to mismatched key. An active attacker cannot forge the signature without A1's private signing key. Thus A2 can authenticate that N3 originated from A1 and is fresh.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 ->* A1: N4,Message 2 is {exp(g,N6),N4,A2}pk(A1). While it contains A2's identity, the encryption is with A1's public key, so any agent can produce such a ciphertext. An active attacker (who does not possess A1's private key) can intercept message 1, generate his own exp(g,N6') and nonce N4', and send a forged message 2 claiming to be A2. A1 decrypts and accepts N4' as coming from A2, with no additional verification that the sender knows A2's private key or that N4 originated from A2. This violates the authentication goal: A1 believes a nonce chosen by the attacker originates from A2.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *-> A2: N4,A1 returns N4 inside the symmetric encryption {|N4,hash(...)|}exp(exp(g,N5),N6). A2 decrypts with the same DH key (which only A1 and A2 can compute if no man-in-the-middle) and checks that N4 matches the one it sent in message 2. The DH key ensures freshness and binds the message to the current session. An attacker cannot produce a valid symmetric encryption without knowing the DH key, which requires either N5 (from A1) or N6 (from A2) and the cleartext exponentiation. Since message 2 is encrypted to A1, an active attacker cannot obtain A2's N6 to compute the key. Thus A2 can authenticate that N4 came from A1 and is fresh.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The signed message {A2,N3}inv(sk(A1)) provides a non-forgeable binding between A1 and nonce N3. A2 verifies the signature using sk(A1), which guarantees that only A1 could have produced it. Since the goal is weak authentication (non-injective agreement), replay of an old message does not violate the property – the message was indeed sent by A1. Hence, the goal holds in the symbolic model without any attack.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3
A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,A2 receives signed message {A2, N3}inv(sk(A1)). The signature verifies with public key sk(A1) and binds A2's identity and nonce N3. Only A1 can produce this signature, so A2 is assured that A1 sent the message. Weak authentication does not require freshness, so replay of an old message (with the same nonce) would still satisfy that A1 sent that nonce at some point. No reflection or man-in-the-middle attack is possible because the intruder cannot forge the signature or alter the contents without detection. Hence, the goal holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3
A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N3,A1 receives signed message {A1, N3}inv(sk(A2)). The signature verifies with public key sk(A2) and binds A1's identity and the same nonce N3. Only A2 can produce this signature, so A1 is assured that A2 sent the message. Weak authentication does not require freshness, so replay of an old message (with the same nonce) would still satisfy that A2 sent that nonce at some point. The intruder cannot forge or modify the message due to the signature, and no type-flaw or reflection attack is feasible because the message content includes the specific agent names. Thus, the goal holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The protocol lacks a freshness mechanism (e.g., a challenge from A2). An intruder can replay the encrypted message {{A2,N3}inv(sk(A1))}pk(A2) to A2, causing A2 to accept the nonce N3 as originating from a fresh run, even if A1 never sent that message in the current session. This violates weak authentication as A2 cannot verify liveness of A1.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","The nonce N3 is encrypted under A2's public key pk(A2) as part of the message {{A2,N3}inv(sk(A1))}pk(A2). Only A2 possesses the corresponding private decryption key inv(pk(A2)), so the intruder cannot decrypt the ciphertext to learn N3. No other message reveals N3, and there is no other channel that leaks the nonce. Hence secrecy is preserved.",,,,

column2: a2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3
A1 weakly authenticates a2 on N3

column1: ,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,a2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3,The intruder can learn N3 from the cleartext message 2 (A1 -> a2: N3) and replay it to a2, impersonating A1. a2 has no cryptographic binding of the response to the first message, so weak authentication fails.",,,,

column2: a2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3
A1 weakly authenticates a2 on N3

column1: ,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates a2 on N3,A1 receives two signed messages from a2 (messages 1 and 3) that both contain N3 and are verified with a2's public verification key. The signatures are unforgeable, so if A1 completes the protocol, a2 must have been actively involved. No replay or forgery is possible.",,,,

column2: a2 weakly authenticates A1 on N3
A1 weakly authenticates a2 on N3

column1: ,True,,,3,0,2,ATTACK,1,N3 secret between a2,A1","N3 is sent in cleartext in message 2 (A1 -> a2: N3). An eavesdropping intruder learns N3, violating secrecy. The encryption of N3 in message 1 does not protect it once it is revealed in the clear.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates A3 on N5,The first message from A2 to A1 contains hash(N5, ki(A3,A2)) in plaintext. An intruder can intercept this message. Later, when A1 sends N5 to A3, the intruder can impersonate A3 by replaying the captured hash. Since A1 does not verify the identity of the sender of the first message, it accepts the hash as proof of A3's knowledge, defeating authentication.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A3","The symmetric key kc(N5, ki(A3,A2)) used to encrypt N4 is transmitted in plaintext from A2 to A1 in the first message. An intruder can obtain this key and subsequently decrypt {|N4|}kc, revealing the secret N4.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A3: N4,Because the symmetric key kc(N5, ki(A3,A2)) is compromised (sent in plaintext), an intruder can modify the encrypted message {|N4|}kc sent from A1 to A3. The intruder can replace N4 with any value of its choice, and A3 will accept it after decryption, violating the data freshness and authenticity of N4.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is encrypted with pk(A2), so only A2 can decrypt. The intruder does not have inv(pk(A2)), thus cannot learn N3. No other message reveals N3. Secrecy holds.",,,,
,True,,,4,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is encrypted with pk(A1), so only A1 can decrypt. The intruder does not have inv(pk(A1)), thus cannot learn N4. Secrecy holds.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,2,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,The only authentication is the inclusion of static shared secret n5 in the encrypted message. Since n5 is not fresh per session, an intruder can replay an old message {N3,n5}pk(A2), and A2 will accept it as originating from A1 with fresh N3. No freshness guarantee, so a replay attack succeeds.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,3,ATTACK,1,A2 ->* A1: N4,The only authentication is the inclusion of static shared secret n6 in the encrypted message. Since n6 is not fresh per session, an intruder can replay an old message {N4,n6}pk(A1), and A1 will accept it as originating from A2 with fresh N4. No freshness guarantee, so a replay attack succeeds.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,The Diffie-Hellman key derived from exp(g,N5) and exp(g,N6) is authenticated by a3 via MACs under shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3). Only A1 knows N5, and A2 knows N6. The encryption of N4 under this key ensures that only A1 could have produced it. The use of fresh N6 per session prevents replay of old a3 MACs, and the MAC structures include identities and session-specific values, preventing type-flaw and reflection attacks. Hence, the authentication goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is encrypted under the DH key exp(exp(g,N5),N6), which is known only to A1 and A2 due to the authenticated DH exchange. The attacker cannot compute this key from the public exponentials without solving the CDH problem, and cannot learn it from other protocol steps because the symmetric keys shk are not compromised. Hence, secrecy is maintained.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N4,All nonces (N5, N6, n8–n12) are static constants known to the intruder. The DH key exp(exp(g,N5),N6) is fixed and computable by the intruder. This allows the intruder to replay the authentication hashes from the trusted server a3 and to encrypt/decrypt the final message. As a result, the intruder can impersonate A1 and inject a chosen N4, so the agreement on N4 between A1 and A2 fails.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,Since the DH key is static and known to the intruder, the intruder can replay the entire protocol trace using constant hashes. After A2 receives the replayed authentication messages, the intruder (impersonating A1) sends {|N4'|}K where K is the known DH key. A2 decrypts and accepts N4' as coming from A1, violating the authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","The DH key exp(exp(g,N5),N6) is static because N5 and N6 are constants known to all agents (including the intruder). The intruder can compute this key and decrypt the message {|N4|}K sent in the last step, thus learning N4. Hence secrecy is lost.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: hash(N3),The protocol sends hash(N3) and N3 in plaintext with no authentication or integrity protection. The intruder can intercept the message, replace both fields with newly computed hash(N') and N', and forward to A2, thereby impersonating A1. Since A2 has no way to verify the origin or freshness of the hash, an authentication attack succeeds.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: hmac(N3,shk(A1,A2))","The protocol has no freshness guarantee (no timestamp, challenge-response, or nonce uniqueness check). An intruder can intercept the message (hmac(N3,shk(A1,A2)), N3) and replay it later to A2. Since A2 cannot distinguish a fresh message from a replayed one, the intruder can impersonate A1 and make A2 believe A1 sent the HMAC, violating the authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates A2 on exp(exp(g,N8),N9)","A1 receives message 4 encrypted with key h(N5,N6,N7,exp(exp(g,N8),N9)). Decryption requires knowledge of the shared secret exp(exp(g,N8),N9). The inner signature by A2 binds the nonces and DH half, preventing impersonation. Fresh nonces N5,N6,N7 prevent replay. The intruder cannot forge A2's signature or compute the shared secret without A2's private exponent.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on exp(exp(g,N8),N9)","A2 receives message 3 encrypted with key h(N5,N6,N7,exp(exp(g,N8),N9)). Decryption requires knowledge of the shared secret. The inner signature by A1 confirms A1's identity and binds the nonces and DH half. Fresh nonces prevent replay. The intruder cannot forge A1's signature or compute the shared secret.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,exp(exp(g,N8),N9) secret between A1,A2","The shared secret exp(exp(g,N8),N9) is never sent in clear. It is used only as input to a one-way function h (key derivation) and as encryption key in message 5. Neither usage reveals the secret value; asymmetric DH ensures only A1 and A2 can compute it from the exchanged exponentials.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,3,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N3,Message 5 encrypts N3 with the shared secret exp(exp(g,N8),N9). Only A1 and A2 know this key (from the secrecy goal). Therefore, A2 can decrypt and be assured that N3 originated from A1. Fresh session key prevents replay across sessions.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K16

Only A1 and a3 know K16 initially; a3 never sends such a message, so A2 can confirm A1's knowledge. Weak authentication permits replay, as the ciphertext still originates from A1. Hence no attack.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K16
A2 weakly authenticates a3 on K16,A1

column1: ,True,,,6,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates A2 on K16,A2's response {|N5,A1,N14|}K16 is encrypted with K16, which A1 knows is fresh from a3 (since it was just decrypted from step2). Thus the message is fresh and proves A2's current knowledge. Replay is impossible because old ciphertexts use different keys. Hence strong authentication holds with no attack.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K16
A2 weakly authenticates a3 on K16,A1

column1: ,True,,,6,0,2,ATTACK,1,A2 *-> A1: K16,Interpreted as strong (injective) authentication of A1 by A2 on K16. A2 has no freshness guarantee: the ticket and {|N4,A2|}K16 from A1 contain no fresh challenge from A2. This breaks injective agreement, thus an attack exists.",,,,

A2 weakly authenticates a3 on K16,A1

A2 can decrypt it and verify that a3 generated K16 for communication with A1. Weak authentication is satisfied even if the message is replayed, because the ciphertext originates from a3. Hence no attack.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K16
A2 weakly authenticates a3 on K16,A1

column1: ,True,,,6,0,4,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K16,A2","The block {|N7,K16,A2,N10|}shk(A1,a3) contains the fresh nonce N7 that A1 sent in step1. This provides a strong, verifiable link to the current run, proving that a3 responded to A1's request. The freshness challenge prevents replay attacks. Hence no attack.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on K16
A2 weakly authenticates a3 on K16,A1

column1: ,True,,,6,0,5,NO_ATTACK,1,K16 secret between A1,A2,a3","K16 is only transmitted encrypted under the symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are known only to the respective agent and a3. The intruder cannot decrypt these ciphertexts, and subsequent messages only use K16 for encryption without revealing it. Hence the key remains secret among the intended parties. No attack.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K16,A1","A2 receives an encryption under the pre-shared key shk(A2,a3) in message 3. This encryption contains the nonce N6 that A2 sent in message 2, along with K16 and A1. Since only a3 knows shk(A2,a3), and the nonce N6 provides freshness, A2 can verify that the message originated from a3 and that a3 generated K16 for A1. No intruder can forge this encryption without knowledge of shk(A2,a3).",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K16,A2","A1 receives an encryption under shk(A1,a3) in message 4 (forwarded by A2). This encryption contains the nonce N4 that A1 sent in message 1, along with K16 and A2. Only a3 knows shk(A1,a3), so A1 can authenticate that a3 generated K16 and intended it for A2. The nonce N4 ensures freshness. Even though the message is forwarded, the encryption prevents tampering.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K16 secret between A1,A2,a3","The session key K16 is generated by a3 and transmitted only within encryptions under the long-term keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3). Only A1 and A2 can decrypt these blocks. Subsequent messages are encrypted with K16, but perfect encryption prevents key leakage. An intruder cannot learn K16 without compromising the long-term keys.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5,The message contains N5 both inside and outside the encryption. In AnB notation, repeated occurrences of the same variable in a message imply that the receiver checks their equality. Therefore, A2 will verify that the cleartext N5 matches the N5 decrypted from {|N3,A2,N5|}shk(A1,A2). Since only A1 and A2 know the symmetric key shk(A1,A2), only A1 could have generated the encryption containing the matching N5. An intruder cannot modify the cleartext N5 without breaking the equality check, and cannot forge the encrypted block. Replay does not violate weak authentication. Thus, the goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 *-> A2: N6,The goal requires A1 to authenticate A2 on the value N6, but A1 never receives N6 from A2 in any protocol step. In message 2, A1 sends N6 in plaintext, but A2 does not send N6 back. Even in a normal execution, A1 has no evidence that A2 knows or has sent N6. The protocol is structurally flawed; a symbolic model checker will find an attack trace (including the normal run) where the goal is violated.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 *-> A1: N8,The goal requires A2 to authenticate A1 on the value N8, but A2 never receives N8 from A1; instead, N8 is generated and sent by A2 in message 3. A1 never sends N8. Thus the protocol does not provide the required exchange to satisfy the goal. A symbolic model checker will identify an attack trace (including the normal run) where the goal is violated.",,,,

Therefore, A2 has no means to check the integrity of the message; an intruder can send any arbitrary value for the f field, causing A2 to accept any N5 as coming from A1. This violates weak authentication.",,,,

Thus weak authentication holds.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *-> A2: N5,The protocol provides A2 with a fresh nonce N3 that is included in the keyed function f computed with the shared key shk(A1,A2) along with A2's identity and N5. A2 can verify the MAC using the shared key and the received N3, A2, N5. Since the key is only known to A1 and A2, and the nonce N3 is freshly generated by A2, the response guarantees that N5 originated from A1 and is fresh. No feasible attack (replay, reflection, type-flaw, or man-in-the-middle) can break this authentication because the intruder cannot forge the MAC without the shared key, and any replayed message would fail due to a mismatching nonce.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5,The goal is weak authentication on nonce N5. The protocol provides A2 with the public key of A1 via a certificate signed by a trusted third party a3. The second message {N4,A2,N5} is signed by A1's private key. Since an adversary cannot forge A1's digital signature without the private key, any valid signature must originate from A1. Therefore, if A2 successfully verifies the signature using the certified public key, it guarantees that A1 has signed that specific message containing N5 at some point. No freshness requirement is imposed by weak authentication, so replay attacks do not violate the goal. No type-flaw or man-in-the-middle attack exists due to the cryptographic binding and the use of asymmetric signatures.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N6
A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N8

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N6,The signed message {N4,A2,N6,n11}inv(sk(A1)) is received by A2 and verified using A1's public key extracted from the certificate {A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)). Since the intruder cannot forge A1's signature (lacking inv(sk(A1))), any valid message containing N6 must have been signed by A1, satisfying weak authentication. Replaying an old signed message does not violate weak authentication because the message still originates from A1.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N6
A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N8

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N8,The signed message {N5,A1,N8}inv(sk(A2)) is received by A1 and verified using A2's public key obtained from the certificate {A2,sk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)). As the intruder lacks inv(sk(A2)), it cannot forge a valid signature containing N8. Therefore, any accepted message with N8 must have been signed by A2, guaranteeing weak authentication. Replayed messages still originate from A2.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N6
A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N8

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N6,The intruder can mount a replay attack: in a previous session, capture message 5 (A1 -> A2: {A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)), {N4,A2,N6}inv(sk(A1))). In a new session, the intruder replays a previous certificate from a3 to A2 (step 3) and the captured message 5 after intercepting A2's outgoing nonce n10 (step 4). A2 then accepts the replayed signed message and believes A1 has sent N6 in the current session, but A1 did not participate in this run. This violates weak authentication.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N6
A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N8

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N8,Similarly, the intruder captures message 6 (A2 -> A1: {A2,sk(A2)}inv(sk(a3)), {N5,A1,N8}inv(sk(A2))) from a previous session. In a new session, the intruder replays a previous certificate from a3 to A1 (step 1) and the captured message 6 after causing A1 to send nonce n10 (step 2) and forcing A1 to receive a replayed n10 (step 4). A1 then accepts the replayed signed message and believes A2 has sent N8 in the current session, but A2 did not participate. This violates weak authentication.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *-> A2: N7,The goal is that A2 authenticates A1 on the value N7. A1 sends to A2 a certificate {A1,sk(A1)}inv(sk(a3)) signed by the trusted authority a3, binding A1's identity to its public verification key sk(A1). Then A1 sends a signed message {N4,N5,A2,N7}inv(sk(A1)). A2 has sk(a3) to verify the certificate, obtains sk(A1), and uses it to verify the signature. The message includes the fresh nonce N5 generated by A2, ensuring freshness, and includes A2's identity, binding the message to this session. Only A1 knows inv(sk(A1)), so the signature cannot be forged. Replay attacks are prevented by the fresh nonce. Hence, A2 can securely conclude that N7 originated from A1 in this run.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4

column1: ,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N4,The symmetric encryption {|N3,A2,N4|}shk(A1,A2) ensures that only A1 and A2 (who share the key) can produce or decrypt the message. The intruder cannot forge or modify the ciphertext. Replay of the message is possible, but since the goal is weak authentication, it only requires that whenever A2 accepts N4 as coming from A1, there exists a run where A1 sent that N4. A replay does not violate this correspondence because the original sender A1 did send the message. Thus, the cryptographic construction prevents any attack that would break the weak authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,2,A1 *-> A2: N6,The goal requires A1 to authenticate A2 on nonce N6. However, N6 is generated by A1 and sent in message 2, but A2 never includes N6 in its response (message 3). Therefore, A1 cannot verify that A2 knows N6. An intruder can replay messages or orchestrate sessions where A1 completes without A2 ever possessing N6, violating the authentication claim.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,2,A2 *-> A1: N8,The goal requires A2 to authenticate A1 on nonce N8. N8 is generated and sent by A2 in message 3, but A1 never echoes N8 back. Hence A2 cannot confirm that A1 actually received or knows N8. The protocol provides no mechanism for A2 to verify A1's knowledge of N8, leading to a detectable attack on the claim.",,,,

column2: A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N7
A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N7,The message containing N7 is encrypted with the symmetric key shk(A1,A2) known only to A1 and A2. The intruder cannot forge or modify this ciphertext. Replay of an old message does not violate weak authentication because A2 indeed sent N7 in the original session. The message structure with 3 fields prevents type-flaw attacks. Therefore, the goal holds.",,,,

column2: A1 weakly authenticates A2 on N7
A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5,The message containing N5 is encrypted with the shared symmetric key shk(A1,A2). Only A1 could have produced this ciphertext. The intruder cannot forge or alter it. Replay attacks do not break weak authentication since A1 actually sent N5. The message structure with 4 fields prevents type-flaw attacks. Thus, the goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *-> A2: N5,The response message {|N3,A2,N5|}shk(A1,A2) is encrypted with the shared key known only to A1 and A2. A2 decrypts it and verifies that his fresh nonce N3 is included, guaranteeing that the message was generated by A1 after A2's challenge. The inclusion of A2's identity prevents reflection attacks. Since the encryption is symmetric and the key is exclusively shared, no other agent can forge a valid response, and replay is prevented by the nonce check. Hence, A2 obtains a fresh and authentic assurance of N5 from A1.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: N3,The protocol sends N3 in plaintext without any encryption or integrity protection. An intruder can eavesdrop the message and learn N3, violating confidentiality. There is no step that ensures secrecy of N3.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,The protocol provides no authentication mechanism (no signatures, no shared secrets, no freshness guarantees). An intruder can impersonate A1 by sending an arbitrary value or replaying an old N3. A2 cannot verify that the message originated from A1, so the authentication goal fails.",,,,
,True,,,2,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: n3,The message n3 is sent in cleartext without any encryption. The Dolev-Yao intruder can intercept and read n3, violating secrecy.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: n3,The message n3 is sent without any authentication mechanism (e.g., signature, MAC, or challenge-response). The intruder can impersonate A1 by replaying or forging the message, so A2 cannot verify the origin.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,a4 *->* A1: N7,The session key K13, used to encrypt the final message from a4 to A1, is also known to a3 (it is established in step 4 and sent to A1 encrypted under K12, which a3 obtains from the shk(a2,a3)-encrypted block in step 2). Since a3 shares n11 (the other value in the ciphertext) with all agents, a corrupted a3 can encrypt an arbitrary N7 under K13 and send it to A1. A1 cannot distinguish this from a genuine message from a4 because the encryption key is identical. Thus, an intruder controlling a3 can impersonate a4 and violate the stated goal.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K4,A2","A1 receives K4 from a3 in the clear with no authentication mechanism. An intruder can impersonate a3 and send a fabricated K4 to A1, causing A1 to believe it originated from a3.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K4,A1","A2 receives K4 from A1 in the clear without any cryptographic authentication. An intruder can impersonate A1 and send a fake K4 to A2, and A2 has no means to verify that K4 came from a3.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,ATTACK,1,K4 secret between A1,A2,a3","K4 is transmitted in plaintext in both message 2 (a3 -> A1) and message 3 (A1 -> A2). Any eavesdropper can intercept and learn the key, violating secrecy.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K4,A2","No freshness mechanism (nonces or timestamps). The intruder can replay an old response from a3 (containing the two ciphertexts) to a new request from A1, making A1 accept a stale K4 and believe it is fresh from a3. This violates the authentication claim.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K4,A1","A2 receives only the ciphertext forwarded by A1, without any direct evidence from a3. The ciphertext does not bind the identity of A1 (only contains K4), so an intruder can replay an old ciphertext and claim it is from any A1. Also lacks freshness, so A2 cannot ensure a3 is present and the key is fresh.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K4 secret between A1,A2,a3","K4 is always encrypted under symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are only known to the intended parties. The intruder does not possess these keys and cannot decrypt the ciphertexts. Therefore, K4 remains secret from the intruder.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K4,A2","No freshness mechanism (nonce or timestamp) is present in the first two messages. An intruder can replay a previous response from a3 to A1, causing A1 to believe that a3 is currently agreeing on K4 and A2, while a3 may not be participating in the session. Therefore, an OFMC symbolic model checker would detect a replay attack, violating the authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K4,A1","A2 receives only a forwarded encrypted message from A1, with no freshness. An intruder can replay the second ciphertext from a previous session, and A2 cannot verify that a3 intended this message for the current session. Thus OFMC would find a replay attack.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K4 secret between A1,A2,a3","K4 is transmitted encrypted under symmetric keys shared only with the intended agents (shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3)). The intruder does not possess these keys (unless compromised), so K4 remains secret. No attack on secrecy is possible in the symbolic model. OFMC would find no attack.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K4, A2","The message {|K4,A1,A2|}shk(A1,a3) provides authenticity (since only A1 and a3 know the key) but no freshness mechanism. An intruder can replay this ciphertext to A1, causing A1 to believe that a3 has initiated a new session with the same K4 and A2, violating injective authentication.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K4, A1","The message {|K4,A1,A2|}shk(A2,a3) is forwarded by A1 to A2. Since the ciphertext is encrypted with shk(A2,a3), it cannot be forged by the intruder, but it can be replayed. An intruder can intercept this ciphertext and later replay it to A2 (possibly impersonating A1), making A2 accept a stale session, thus violating authentication.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K4 secret between A1, A2, a3","K4 is transmitted only inside ciphertexts encrypted with symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are known solely to the intended agents. The intruder cannot decrypt these ciphertexts, and no protocol step reveals K4 in the clear. Hence the secrecy goal holds in the symbolic model.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2","The protocol lacks a freshness mechanism (nonce or timestamp) from A1 to a3. An intruder can replay an old message 2 (the ciphertext encrypted with shk(A1,a3)) from a previous session, causing A1 to believe that a3 sent K6 and A2 in the current session. This violates the authentication goal as a3 never sent that message in this run.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1","Similarly, A2 receives the key via message 3 forwarded by A1, but there is no freshness guarantee. An intruder replaying an old message 3 (the ciphertext encrypted with shk(A2,a3)) can make A2 accept an old K6 and A1 as coming from a3 in the current session, breaking authentication.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K6 secret between A1,A2,a3","K6 is generated by a3 and transmitted only encrypted under the long-term symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are unknown to the intruder. The subsequent nonce exchange (messages 4 and 5) is encrypted with K6 and does not leak the key. Even a replay attack using an old K6 does not reveal that key to the intruder, as the old K6 was also protected by the same long-term keys. Thus, the intruder cannot obtain K6, and the secrecy goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2","The message from a3 to A1 (step 2) contains the nonce N4 that A1 previously sent, ensuring freshness. The message is encrypted under the symmetric key shk(A1,a3) which is only known to A1 and a3, preventing impersonation. Thus, A1 can be confident that a3 generated K6 and intended A2 as the other party. No attack trace exists.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1","The ticket received by A2 (step 3) contains no freshness indicator from A2. An attacker can replay an old ticket from a previous session. A2 will decrypt the ticket, associate K6 and A1 with a3, but the ticket may be stale. This violates the freshness requirement for authentication, so an attack exists.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K6 secret between A1,A2,a3","K6 is transmitted only inside encrypted messages: first under shk(A1,a3) to A1, then under shk(A2,a3) in the ticket. The subsequent use of K6 for encrypting N5 and pre(N5) does not leak the key. Since the encryption is perfect in the symbolic model, no attacker can learn K6. Therefore, the secrecy goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2","Nonce N4 is sent in cleartext in message 2. The intruder can learn N4, then start a new session with a3 impersonating A1 using the same N4. a3 generates a fresh K6' and returns {|K6',A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3). The intruder forwards this block to A1, who decrypts it, sees its own N4, and accepts it as a valid response from a3. This breaks the binding between nonce and session, allowing A1 to authenticate a3 on a key that was generated for a different session.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1","Nonce N5 is sent in cleartext in messages 1 and 2. The intruder can learn N5, then start a new session with a3 impersonating A1 (or use a parallel session) using a fresh N4 and the stolen N5. a3 generates a new K6'' and returns {|K6'',A1,N5|}shk(A2,a3). The intruder forwards this block to A2, who decrypts it, sees its own N5, and accepts it as a valid response from a3. This violates the freshness guarantee and allows A2 to authenticate a3 on a key from a different run.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K6 secret between A1,A2,a3","K6 is always transmitted encrypted under keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are known only to A1, A2, and a3. The intruder does not possess these keys and cannot decrypt the ciphertexts. No protocol action exposes K6 in cleartext or via other means, so secrecy is preserved.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2","A1 receives {|K6,A1,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) containing fresh nonce N4, ensuring a3 is live and intended key K6 for A2. No attacker can forge this ciphertext without key shk(A1,a3).",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1","A2 receives {|K6,A1,A2,N5|}shk(A2,a3) via A1, but A1 cannot modify it. Fresh nonce N5 ensures a3's liveness and intent. No forgery possible.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K6 secret between A1,A2,a3","K6 is encrypted with symmetric keys only known to A1, A2, and a3, and each ciphertext conveys K6 to the intended recipient. No other agent can decrypt without the keys.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,3,A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2","A1 receives from a3 an encryption of (K6,A1,A2,N4) under shk(A1,a3), which only a3 can produce. The nonce N4 provides freshness and is confirmed by matching the one A1 generated. No reflection or replay is possible due to key separation and nonce binding.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,3,A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1","A2 receives via A1 an encryption of (K6,A1,A2,N5) under shk(A2,a3), which only a3 can produce. The nonce N5 is fresh and matches the one A2 sent. Even if A1 is compromised, the encryption cannot be forged, and replay fails due to nonce uniqueness.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,3,K6 secret between A1,A2,a3","K6 is transmitted only in two symmetric encryptions: one under shk(A1,a3) and one under shk(A2,a3). Only the key-holders can decrypt, so the intruder cannot learn K6. No information leakage occurs.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K6,A2","A1 receives {|K6,A2,N4|}shk(A1,a3) which includes its fresh nonce N4. Only a3 knows shk(A1,a3), so the message must originate from a3. This provides strong authentication of a3 on K6 and A2.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K6,A1","A2 receives {|K6,A1,N5,a3|}shk(A2,a3) forwarded by A1. The nonce N5 was generated by A2 and is fresh. Only a3 knows shk(A2,a3), so the message must come from a3. Thus A2 authenticates a3 on K6 and A1.",,,,

,True,,,3,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,K6 secret between A1,A2,a3","K6 is transmitted only under symmetric encryption with keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3), which are known only to the intended parties (A1, a3, A2). The intruder cannot decrypt these ciphertexts, so K6 remains secret among A1, A2, and a3.",,,,

,True,,,1,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: N4,The first message transmits N4 in cleartext. The goal declares N4 as a secret between A1 and A2, but the intruder can passively eavesdrop and learn N4, directly violating secrecy.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","The nonce N4 is transmitted exclusively in ciphertexts encrypted with the recipient's public key (A2 for the first message, A1 for the second). Only the legitimate agents possess the corresponding private keys, so the intruder cannot decrypt these messages. No other protocol step reveals N4 in plaintext or permits algebraic deduction. Hence secrecy holds.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N5 secret between A1,A2","The nonce N5 is transmitted exclusively in ciphertexts encrypted with the recipient's public key (A2 for the third message, A1 for the fourth). Only the legitimate agents possess the corresponding private keys, so the intruder cannot decrypt these messages. No other protocol step reveals N5 in plaintext or permits algebraic deduction. Hence secrecy holds.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,4,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 *->* A1: N5,N5 is only transmitted encrypted under the session key K7, which is itself protected by long-term symmetric keys shk(A1,a3) and shk(A2,a3). Since the intruder does not know these keys and K7 is never disclosed in plaintext, N5 remains secret between A2 and A1. No attack on secrecy exists in the symbolic model.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,4,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5,After A2 sends N5 encrypted under K7, it receives back pre(N5) encrypted under K7. Only A1 (and a3, who generated K7) can compute this response because K7 is secret and protected. The intruder cannot forge the encryption without K7, and replaying old responses would involve different nonces. Thus, A2 obtains weak agreement that A1 is alive and participated in the run.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,4,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates a3 on K7,A1 includes a fresh nonce N4 in the first message and receives it back in the response encrypted with shk(A1,a3), along with K7 and the ticket. Only a3 knows this long-term key, so the response proves a3's presence and timeliness. The nonce provides freshness, ensuring the key K7 is generated in the current session. No replay or impersonation is possible.",,,,

column2: A2 weakly authenticates A1 on N5

column1: ,True,,,4,0,3,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on K7,A2 receives the ticket {|K7, A1|}shk(A2,a3) from A1 without any freshness mechanism (e.g., a nonce challenge). An intruder can replay a previously intercepted ticket from a legitimate run, causing A2 to believe that a3 generated K7 in the current session, even though a3 may not be active. This is a classic replay attack against the authentication goal.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N3,The protocol includes A1's identity in the first message encrypted with A2's public key, ensuring only A2 can decrypt and associate N3 with A1. The second message echoes N3 and includes A2's identity and a fresh nonce N4, encrypted with A1's public key. The third message returns N4 encrypted with A2's public key, proving possession of A1's private key. This mutual challenge-response prevents replay and man-in-the-middle attacks, securely authenticating A1 to A2 on N3.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 *->* A1: N4,The second message contains N4 and A2's identity, encrypted with A1's public key, so only A1 can decrypt and learn N4. The third message returns N4 encrypted with A2's public key, confirming that A1 decrypted the second message. Since only A2 could have created the second message (it includes the decryption of the first message to obtain N3), A1 is assured that N4 originated from A2. The use of fresh nonces and identity binding prevents type-flaw and parallel session attacks.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,A2 completes authentication only after receiving {N5}pk(A2). This final message cannot be forged by the intruder because it requires knowledge of N5, which is encrypted in step 6 under A1's public key, and the intruder does not have A1's private key. Only A1 can decrypt step 6 and produce the correct response. Therefore, the authentication goal holds.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates A2 on N5,A1 receives N5 in step 6 encrypted with its public key, including A1's own nonce N4. The intruder cannot forge this message because it would need to know N4 (which is sent encrypted to A2) and the correct N5 (chosen by A2). Replay fails due to N4 mismatch. Thus, only A2 can generate a valid message, so authentication is secure.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is transmitted in step 3 encrypted with A2's public key and in step 6 encrypted with A1's public key. The intruder does not possess the private keys of A1 or A2, so it cannot decrypt these messages. N4 remains confidential.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,3,NO_ATTACK,1,N5 secret between A1,A2",N5 is transmitted in step 6 encrypted with A1's public key and in step 7 encrypted with A2's public key. The intruder lacks the corresponding private keys and cannot decrypt. N5 remains confidential.,,,,

,True,,,6,0,4,ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N4,This non-injective agreement is violated because the intruder can send an arbitrary nonce N4' to A2 encrypted with A2's public key (which is publicly known). A2 decrypts and accepts the message as coming from A1, even though A1 never generated that N4'. The message is not bound to A1's identity by any cryptographic guarantee.",,,,

,True,,,6,0,5,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 *->* A1: N5,The message containing N5 (step 6) is encrypted with A1's public key and includes the fresh nonce N4 from A1. The intruder cannot forge this message without knowing N4 (which remains secret) or by replaying an old message (which would have a mismatching N4). Thus, any N5 received by A1 must originate from A2.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,0,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,The protocol is structurally identical to the Needham-Schroeder Public Key Protocol, which is vulnerable to a man-in-the-middle attack. An intruder I can impersonate A1 to A2 by first initiating a session with A1 (pretending to be A2) to obtain a nonce, then using that nonce in a session with A2 as A1. A2 will then complete the run believing it has authenticated A1 on the nonce N3, but N3 was actually generated by A1 in a different session and is known to the intruder.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,1,ATTACK,1,A1 authenticates A2 on N4,In the same man-in-the-middle attack, A1 can be convinced that it is communicating with A2. The intruder forwards A2's encrypted response (containing N4) to A1, making A1 believe it originated from A2. However, the intruder is actively manipulating the messages, so A1's authentication of A2 on N4 is invalid.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,2,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is transmitted only inside encryptions: first encrypted with A2's public key (step 1), and later reused inside encryption with A1's public key (step 2). The intruder cannot decrypt these messages without the corresponding private keys, so N3 remains secret from the intruder.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,3,NO_ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is sent only inside encryptions: first encrypted with A1's public key in step 2, and then encrypted with A2's public key in step 3. The intruder cannot decrypt these without the private keys, thus N4 remains secret.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 *->* A2: N3,The nonce N3 is generated by A1 and sent encrypted under the shared long-term key shk(A1,A2) in message 2. ,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,A2 *-> A1: N4,The nonce N4 is generated by A2 and sent in the clear in message 1. In message 2, A1 sends f(N4) encrypted under the shared key shk(A1,A2). ,True,,,13,0,10,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates a3 on N8,N5,N6,hmac(N7,Hx5A2N7),hmac(pan(A1,a3),Hx5a3panA1a3),N4","A2 receives a signed message from a3 (message 9) that includes N5,N6,N4 and the HMAC values, but does NOT include N8. ,True,,,5,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,The second message sends N3 and N4 in plaintext, allowing an intruder to learn them. ,True,,,5,0,2,ATTACK,1,N3,N4 secret between A1,A2","A2 sends N3 and N4 in plaintext in the second message. The intruder can intercept this message and learn both nonces, violating their secrecy.",,,,
,True,,,5,0,3,ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 is sent in plaintext in the second message. The intruder can intercept it and learn N3, violating its secrecy.",,,,
,True,,,5,0,4,ATTACK,1,N4 secret between A1,A2","N4 is sent in plaintext in the second message. The intruder can intercept it and learn N4, violating its secrecy.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 -> A2: N3,N4","The message is sent in plaintext without any integrity or authentication mechanism. An active intruder can intercept the transmission and replace N3,N4 with arbitrary values, so A2 cannot be sure it received exactly what A1 sent.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,1,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,N4","Similar to the previous goal, the '*' indicates eventual delivery but does not add security. The message is unprotected, so an intruder can modify it en route, violating the goal that A2 receives the original N3,N4.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,2,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,This goal concerns only N3, which is sent as part of the plaintext message. Without any protection, an intruder can alter or replace N3 arbitrarily, breaking the claim that A2 receives the intended value from A1.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,3,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,N4","Authentication requires evidence that the claimed origin (A1) is indeed the source. ,True,,,2,0,0,NO_ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,The only message is {N3}pk(A2). Since A2's private key is only known to A2, the intruder cannot decrypt and learn N3. No other message reveals N3. Therefore, secrecy of N3 between A1 and A2 is preserved.",,,,

,True,,,2,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2",The encryption with pk(A2) ensures that only A2 can decrypt N3. A1 knows N3 by construction. The intruder cannot obtain N3. Hence the secrecy goal holds.,,,,

,True,,,4,0,0,ATTACK,1,A1 ->* A2: N3,The intruder can replay a previous second message from A1 to A2, causing A2 to believe that A1 sent N3 in a new run. Since the protocol lacks binding to a fresh challenge (N4 is not authenticated), the goal is violated.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,1,NO_ATTACK,1,N3 secret between A1,A2","N3 appears only in the second message encrypted with A2's public key. Only A2 can decrypt this message, and the intruder cannot obtain N3 from the other message. The symbolic model ensures confidentiality.",,,,

,True,,,4,0,2,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N3,A2 has no way to verify that N3 came from A1 in response to its own challenge. ,True,,,4,0,3,ATTACK,1,A2 authenticates A1 on N4,The intruder can replay an old response from A1 containing N4, or can act as a man-in-the-middle: intercept A2's first message, send a fake first message to A1 with a different N4_I, and forward the response to A2.
